description: The figure presents SEM characterization of crack paths and fracture surface morphologies in multiphase low-density steels subjected to different intercritical annealing temperatures, specifically AN750-T, AN800-T, and AN850-T (annealed at 750 °C, 800 °C, and 850 °C respectively, followed by tempering at 400 °C). Panel (a) shows microcrack initiation and propagation: microvoids (yellow dashed circles) nucleate predominantly at interfaces between soft and hard domains. In AN750-T and AN800-T, crack tips blunt at hard-domain interfaces and generate secondary cracks (yellow arrows), whereas in AN850-T the hard domains are severed (red arrows), indicating increased brittleness. Panel (b) displays the central fracture zone morphology, where AN750-T exhibits deep dimples indicative of ductile fracture, while AN800-T and AN850-T show quasi-cleavage features (red dashed outlines) originating from fresh martensite alongside dimples. Panel (c) provides bimodal dimple size distributions, with average sizes decreasing from 1.54 μm (AN750-T) to 1.31 μm (AN850-T), and peak positions shifting to smaller values. Collectively, the figure demonstrates that higher annealing temperatures promote brittle fracture mechanisms by hard-domain severance and reduced dimple coalescence, undermining the stress-buffering capacity of the multiphase microstructure.
